articles.title: Ether climbs toward $2K as Bitmine buys ETH, Robinhood L2 boost
articles.summary: ETH charts a path toward $2,000 as TradFi adoption, DAT buying and a long-awaited network upgrade boost investor interest.
articles.content:
Ether climbs toward $2K as Bitmine buys ETH, Robinhood L2 boost

ETH charts a path toward $2,000 as TradFi adoption, DAT buying and a long-awaited network upgrade boost investor interest.

Key takeaways:

  • BitMine’s aggressive ETH accumulation has reinforced the $1,500 support despite $8B unrealized losses.
  • Glamsterdam upgrade and Robinhood Chain launch signal stronger TradFi focus for Ethereum’s base layer.

Ether (ETH) price rallied 15% in five days, distancing itself from the $1,500 low hit on June 26. Part of the improvement in investor sentiment can be pinned to the final tests on Ethereum’s Glamsterdam upgrade, targeted for later in 2026. Moreover, BitMine Immersion Technologies' continued Ether accumulation helped strengthen the support level. Will $2,000 come next?

Total crypto capitalization/USD (left) vs. ETH/USD (right). Source: TradingView

Ether outperformed the total crypto market capitalization by 7% over the past 30 days. Some excitement came from optimism about the passage of the Digital Assets CLARITY Act, a bill that has faced several hurdles advancing in Congress after pushback from the banking sector on stablecoin regulation and potential rewards to its holders.

ETH options 25% delta skew (put-call) at Deribit. Source: Laevitas

Ether’s recent rally toward $1,800 was enough to instill some confidence in ETH options markets as the skew (put-call) metric exited the fear levels that prevailed until Friday. The current 9% premium in put (sell) options relative to equivalent call (buy) instruments is far from bullish, but it distances itself from the 15% mark from the prior week. Levels above 12% typically indicate extreme fear.

Ethereum network upgrade, Robinhood Chain behind ETH price rally

One of Ethereum’s main criticisms lies in its scalability using layer-2 rollups facilitated by data packages (blobs) that drastically reduced transaction fees but triggered fierce debates over long-term data censorship and centralization. Base layer network fees took a hit, which in turn reduced ETH burning and ultimately led to inflationary supply dynamics.

Ethereum base layer monthly network revenue, USD. Source: DefiLlama

The Ethereum Glamsterdam upgrade, currently in the testing phase, should improve network processing speeds by allowing more transactions to be processed in parallel. The proposal will also expand capacity for Ethereum to handle more data at higher throughput and reduce database bloat. One goal is providing institutional-grade infrastructure for financial use cases.

BitMine (BMNR US) ETH holdings and shares outstanding. Source: bmnr.rocks

The continued accumulation by the US-listed company BitMine Immersion has likely helped strengthen the $1,500 support. The company increased its holdings by 325,000 ETH over the past month, boosting its reserves to 5.74 million ETH. Regardless of the current $8 billion in unrealized losses on its ETH holdings, BitMine continues its path toward acquiring 5% of the existing supply.

Related: Vitalik Buterin shares priorities for new 'Lean Ethereum' strawmap

The launch of Robinhood Chain on July 2, an EVM-compatible Ethereum layer-2 built using Arbitrum technology, helped consolidate the ecosystem with the traditional finance industry. More importantly, Robinhood rolled out tokenized stock trading in more than 120 countries along with major decentralized finance (DeFi) integrations, including Uniswap, 1inch and Morpho.

While Ethereum onchain and derivatives metrics paint a somewhat bearish picture with low network fees and low conviction in options markets, Ether’s upside comes from real-world traditional finance use case growth and network upgrades capable of significantly expanding base layer capacity. Overall, the path to ETH at $2,000 appears entirely viable in the near term.

More on the subject

articles.title: Home Depot Inc balances housing headwinds and pro demand. HD stock reflects a mature retail cycle
articles.summary: Home Depot Inc faces the challenge of balancing softer DIY demand due to housing affordability issues with strong demand from professional customers. The company's stock reflects a mature retail cycle, where its ability to innovate in services, technology, and product assortme...
articles.content:
Home Depot Inc (ISIN US4370761029) is one of the largest home improvement retailers in the United States, with its primary listing on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker HD. The company operates a broad network of big-box stores serving both DIY homeowners and professional contractors, and its stock is commonly included in major US equity benchmarks such as the Dow Jones Industrial Average. For investors, the interplay between housing affordability, renovation activity, and professional construction demand has become central to the Home Depot Inc story.

Demand shaped by housing and renovation cycles

Home Depot Inc generates the majority of its revenue by supplying building materials, tools, and home improvement products to customers who are upgrading, maintaining, or constructing residential properties. Sales volumes are closely tied to housing market trends, including home sales, remodeling activity, and household formation. In periods when mortgage rates and house prices make new purchases more difficult, spending often shifts toward smaller-scale upgrades and maintenance projects instead of large renovations or move-in overhauls.

DIY demand tends to be more sensitive to consumer confidence and budget constraints than professional contractor demand. When economic uncertainty rises or borrowing costs are elevated, some households delay discretionary projects, focusing on essential repairs rather than cosmetic improvements. That can lead to a mix of transactions with a higher proportion of must-do categories such as plumbing, electrical, and basic maintenance supplies, while categories like decor or high-end fixtures may slow.

Professional contractor demand, by contrast, often benefits from longer project pipelines and multi-month contracts tied to remodeling, small residential construction, or light commercial work. Many professionals rely on large home improvement retailers as a primary source for lumber, building materials, hardware, and tools, making consistent store availability and inventory management critical to their operations. This segment can provide a stabilizing effect for Home Depot Inc when DIY traffic moderates, as contractors still need materials to complete jobs already under way.

Operating strategy and store network

Home Depot Inc operates thousands of stores across the United States and select international markets, typically located in high-traffic retail corridors or near highway access. The store format is designed around large aisles and palletized inventory, allowing customers to buy in bulk and find products across multiple categories efficiently. Layouts are organized by project type, such as flooring, kitchen, bath, garden, tools, and building materials, to help both homeowners and professionals navigate quickly.

Inventory management is a core operational priority. Home Depot Inc must balance stocking enough product variety and volume to meet demand without tying up excessive working capital in slow-moving items. Seasonal rotation is a recurring challenge, as categories like outdoor living, garden supplies, and heating products have distinct peaks and troughs throughout the year. Efficient logistics and distribution centers underpin this model, supporting frequent replenishment and enabling stores to adjust stock levels as demand patterns change.

The company has spent years refining its supply chain, deploying forecasting systems and regional distribution hubs to reduce out-of-stocks and shorten delivery times. This is particularly important for professional customers who need predictable availability of key materials to keep job sites on schedule. Many stores also execute special orders through centralized systems, enabling customers to access a broader assortment than what is physically present in each location.

Labor management and in-store expertise are additional elements of the operating strategy. Home Depot Inc trains associates to handle project questions, advise on tools, and support customers in selecting compatible materials for plumbing, electrical, or structural work. For complex projects, staff assistance can influence which retailer customers choose, making service quality a contributor to market share, not just price and product assortment.

Home Depot Inc and digital integration

Home Depot Inc has invested heavily in digital capabilities to complement its physical stores. Customers can browse product assortments online, check local store availability, and place orders for in-store pickup or delivery. This omnichannel approach is designed to meet different shopping preferences, ranging from homeowners who want quick pick-up of a few items to professionals who need bulk delivery directly to job sites.

Online tools often include product reviews, installation guides, and project inspiration content, supporting customers who are planning renovations or repairs. The integration between digital platforms and store inventory systems allows users to see whether items are in stock at nearby locations. For busy contractors, the ability to schedule pickups or deliveries efficiently can reduce time spent away from job sites.

Home Depot Inc has also expanded its use of data analytics to understand buying patterns, optimize assortment, and structure promotions. Loyalty programs and professional customer accounts provide insight into repeat purchasing behavior, enabling tailored offers and communications for high-value segments. This helps the company focus on categories where demand is resilient and where customers demonstrate strong repeat engagement.

Professional customers and services

Professional customers constitute a significant share of revenue for Home Depot Inc. These customers include general contractors, electricians, plumbers, remodelers, and maintenance professionals who need reliable sources of supplies. To support them, the company offers specialized services such as bulk purchasing options, job-site delivery, tool rental, and dedicated pro check-out areas in many stores.

Tool rental programs allow contractors and serious DIY customers to access equipment without the upfront cost of ownership. This can be particularly attractive for tools that are expensive or used only occasionally, such as heavy-duty saws, floor polishers, or demolition equipment. Home Depot Inc benefits through ancillary rental revenue and increased foot traffic, while customers gain flexibility and cost control.

Job-site delivery is another key service, enabling large orders of lumber, drywall, roofing materials, and other bulk products to be transported directly to construction or renovation projects. This reduces logistical burden on contractors and helps ensure that materials arrive in time to keep work progressing. Reliable delivery scheduling becomes part of the value proposition, complementing pricing and product range.

Competitive position in home improvement retail

Home Depot Inc competes with other major home improvement chains, regional hardware stores, specialty retailers, and online marketplaces. Scale is a major advantage, allowing the company to negotiate with suppliers, manage distribution networks, and invest in technology at levels that smaller rivals may find difficult. High store density also supports brand recognition and customer convenience, as many households live within a reasonable driving distance of a Home Depot Inc store.

Product breadth is another competitive factor. In addition to core building materials and tools, Home Depot Inc carries appliances, lighting, storage solutions, garden equipment, and other categories that appeal to homeowners improving multiple aspects of their living spaces. This one-stop-shop positioning can generate incremental basket size, as shoppers add complementary items while purchasing materials for a main project.

Pricing strategies typically aim to remain competitive while preserving margins through private-label brands, vendor agreements, and category management. Home Depot Inc often carries exclusive product lines or store brands that differentiate its offerings and provide higher margin opportunities. In categories where pricing pressure is intense, such as certain commodity materials, efficiency gains in logistics and operations help sustain profitability.

Home Depot Inc product focus - tools and building materials

A representative product category for Home Depot Inc is power tools and hand tools, which are essential for both professionals and dedicated DIY customers. The company offers a wide selection of drills, saws, sanders, nailers, and measuring devices, alongside basic hand tools such as hammers, wrenches, screwdrivers, and pliers. These products are often sold across multiple brands and performance tiers so that casual users and experienced tradespeople can find suitable options.

Power tools are frequently paired with accessories like drill bits, blades, and batteries, creating recurring demand as customers replace worn components or upgrade to new systems. Retailers like Home Depot Inc benefit from these repeat purchases, especially when customers commit to specific battery platforms that encourage ongoing purchases within the same ecosystem. In stores, dedicated tool aisles display equipment and accessories together, making it easier for shoppers to understand compatibility and assemble complete kits.

Beyond tools, building materials represent another cornerstone of Home Depot Inc's assortment. Lumber, plywood, drywall, roofing products, insulation, and concrete-related items form the backbone of structural projects. Organized yard areas and indoor material aisles accommodate large carts and forklifts, enabling both professionals and homeowners to load significant quantities. For many customers, the ability to obtain all major materials for a renovation or small construction project in one visit is a key draw.

Home Depot Inc stock and market perception

Home Depot Inc stock reflects how investors judge the balance between consumer-driven DIY spending, professional contractor demand, and the broader economic cycle. In periods of strong housing activity and favorable credit conditions, market participants often expect robust sales growth from renovation and improvement projects. Conversely, when housing transactions slow or affordability is stretched, expectations may shift toward more measured growth, with professional and maintenance categories carrying a larger share of the revenue mix.

Analysts frequently examine metrics such as comparable store sales, average ticket size, online penetration, and pro-customer share to assess performance. These indicators help clarify whether growth is driven by traffic increases, higher spending per visit, digital channels, or deeper engagement among professional customers. Operating margin trends and inventory levels also feature in such assessments, as they indicate how well Home Depot Inc is managing costs and balancing its stock against demand.

Because Home Depot Inc is widely held and tracked, its stock is often used as a barometer of US housing and renovation activity. Changes in expectations for consumer spending, interest rates, and construction labor availability can all influence sentiment. For long-term investors, the company's ability to sustain dividends, invest in infrastructure, and adapt to evolving customer behavior plays a large role in portfolio decisions, alongside valuation considerations.

Overall, Home Depot Inc occupies a central position in the US home improvement ecosystem. Its scale, store network, professional services, and digital integration give it substantial leverage over how households and contractors source materials and tools. For the stock, the key challenge is navigating the mature phase of the retail cycle while continuing to innovate in service, technology, and product assortment.

articles.title: Alliant Energy Corp outlines its regulated utility path. LNT focuses on stable returns for investors
articles.summary: Alliant Energy Corp (LNT) operates as a regulated electric and gas utility, deriving stable and predictable returns from its Midwest infrastructure investments and customer base. The company's strategy focuses on steady capital investment in grid modernization, safety improvem...
articles.content:
Alliant Energy Corp (ISIN US0188021085) is a regulated utility holding company known under the ticker LNT, providing electric and natural gas service to residential, commercial and industrial customers in the US Midwest. The company operates through utility subsidiaries that focus on delivering reliable energy, maintaining critical infrastructure and earning an allowed return on equity under state regulation. For investors, the core appeal of LNT lies in its predictable business model and a dividend-oriented capital allocation strategy typical for regulated utilities.

Regulated utility framework

Alliant Energy Corp centers its business on regulated electric and gas distribution, which means most of its earnings are determined by approved rates set by state utility commissions. These rate cases usually allow the company to recover prudently incurred costs for fuel, operations, maintenance and new investments, along with an authorized profit margin. As a result, revenues and cash flows tend to be more stable than in many unregulated sectors, even when broader economic conditions fluctuate.

The company’s utilities serve customers in key Midwestern states, supplying electricity over transmission and distribution networks and delivering natural gas through pipeline systems. Customer demand is driven primarily by residential usage for heating, cooling and everyday appliances, supplemented by commercial and industrial loads that depend on local manufacturing and service activity. Seasonal patterns, such as higher consumption during cold winters and hot summers, influence volumes but are moderated by the regulatory framework that spreads costs over time.

Investment focus and strategic priorities

Alliant Energy Corp’s long-term strategy typically emphasizes steady capital investment in its grids, generation assets and service quality. Utility capital expenditure programs often include modernizing transmission lines, upgrading substations, improving gas pipeline safety and expanding capacity where customer growth requires it. These projects are generally added to the regulated rate base, supporting future earnings growth as regulators allow the company to earn a return on its invested capital.

Analysts covering regulated utilities commonly highlight three pillars for companies like Alliant Energy Corp: reliability, affordability and sustainability. Reliability involves minimizing outages and enhancing resilience against storms and extreme weather through network hardening and smarter grid technologies. Affordability means working to keep customer bills reasonable while still funding necessary infrastructure upgrades. Sustainability increasingly includes the shift toward cleaner generation resources, more efficient operations and support for customer energy efficiency programs.

Representative business segment

One representative part of Alliant Energy Corp’s business is its regulated electric utility operations. articles.title: Sticky Inflation Keeps Income Central to the Bond Market Case
articles.summary: Wall Street ends lower, Nasdaq drops 1% as AI trade takes a hit, oil prices spike Over the first half of 2026, markets faced some expected — and unexpected — tailwinds and headwinds, ranging from geopolitical developments, blockbuster corporate earnings, increasing artificial...
articles.content:
Wall Street ends lower, Nasdaq drops 1% as AI trade takes a hit, oil prices spike

Over the first half of 2026, markets faced some expected — and unexpected — tailwinds and headwinds, ranging from geopolitical developments, blockbuster corporate earnings, increasing artificial intelligence (AI) scrutiny, resilient economic data, and a new Federal Reserve (Fed) Chair. In the recently released Midyear Outlook 2026: Policy, Buildouts, & Bottlenecks, we discuss what’s shaping the economic and market landscape and what investors may expect over the balance of the year.

Here are some of the key takeaways from the just-released report.

Economy

We believe economic growth will moderate but remain positive over the second half as strong business investment should offset weakness in housing and rate-sensitive sectors. If geopolitical tensions ease meaningfully, upward pressure on inflation should lose one of its main near-term accelerants while domestic demand and labor-market conditions become less inflationary. We expect unemployment to edge higher but remain historically low as hiring remains subdued.

Stocks

An improving but still challenging macro backdrop and AI-driven earnings strength will continue to be the key pillars of support that will help the broader equity advance continue through year-end. Risks remain, however, as developments in the Middle East and AI monetization progress will determine the magnitude and frequency of potential pullbacks. We expect modest equity market gains in the second half, and maintain a moderate equity overweight to take advantage — but staying diversified between the AI theme and potential rotation beneficiaries could be critical.

Bonds

Sticky inflation and resilient growth are likely to keep the Fed on an extended pause, leaving Treasury yields range-bound, with the 10-year expected to finish the year between 4.00% and 4.50%. In this environment, bond market returns may remain primarily driven by income, and we favor owning core bond sectors over lower-quality riskier sectors. Longer-term-oriented investors should continue to benefit from extending cash maturities slightly to capture still elevated bond yields, as we believe the Fed will eventually continue its rate-cutting campaign.

Alternative Investments

Markets are increasingly being shaped by dispersion, structural change, and rising complexity, which reinforces the important role of alternatives in enhancing portfolio flexibility, diversification, and return potential. Elevated single-stock volatility in the second half should continue to benefit long/short equity strategies, while diverging monetary policies and ongoing geopolitical uncertainty will create a constructive backdrop for global macro.

Commodities and Currencies

Oil volatility should remain elevated as inventories stay tight and supply recovers gradually. In metals, gold’s long-term fundamentals remain favorable despite weaker technicals, while industrial metals are benefiting from infrastructure and AI-driven demand amid constrained supply. Plus, the trend towards resource nationalism will broadly support key commodities. On the dollar, the greenback remains range-bound but supported by U.S. economic resilience, AI-led equity gains, favorable interest rate differentials, and a still-intact secular uptrend.

Four Key Themes to Watch

We see four themes shaping markets in the second half. First, U.S. midterms could shift congressional control and policy direction. Second, rising resource nationalism is steering capital toward commodities and real assets and shifting the geopolitical landscape. Third, AI is maturing, and focus is likely to move from buildout to beneficiaries and return on investment. Finally, a leadership change at the Fed adds uncertainty: with inflation elevated and geopolitics tense, the new chair will have little margin for error.

Equifax Announces Earnings Release Date and Conference Call for Second Quarter 2026 Results

ATLANTA, July 7, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Equifax® (NYSE: EFX) will announce its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30, 2026, in a release to be issued on Tuesday, July 21, at 6:30 a.m. Eastern Time (ET).

Equifax will host a conference call at 8:30 a.m. ET on July 21, in which senior management will discuss financial and business results for the quarter. Related presentation materials will be published on investor.equifax.com on July 21 at 6:30 a.m. ET.

Conference Call:
US/Canada: 877-559-1190 / +1 201-389-0916
International: Click here for participant International Toll-Free access numbers

Please dial the appropriate number 5-10 minutes prior to the call to complete registration. Name and affiliation/company are required to join the call.

Webcast:
To view the webcast and slide presentation, please click the link and enter your information to be connected. The link becomes active 15 minutes prior to the scheduled start time.

An audio replay of the conference call will be available on investor.equifax.com beginning on July 22.

ABOUT EQUIFAX INC.
At Equifax (NYSE: EFX), we believe knowledge drives progress. As a global data, analytics, and technology company, we play an essential role in the global economy by helping financial institutions, companies, employers, and government agencies make critical decisions with greater confidence. Our unique blend of differentiated data, analytics, and cloud technology drives insights to power decisions to move people forward. Headquartered in Atlanta and supported by nearly 15,000 employees worldwide, Equifax operates or has investments in 24 countries in North America, Central and South America, Europe, and the Asia Pacific region.
